title: Tea loaf
subtitle: Fruit soaked in cold tea, baked in a loaf tin
excerpt: Tea loaf is made by soaking dried fruit overnight in cold strong black tea, then binding it with egg and flour the next day. There is no fat in the recipe — the tea-plumped fruit provides all the moisture. It keeps for a week and improves as it sits.
sourceNotes: Tea loaf appears across Welsh, Yorkshire, and Scottish regional cooking under various names: bara brith (Welsh), Yorkshire tea loaf, and Scots black bun share the same technique of soaking dried fruit in liquid before baking. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — tea-soaked fruit cakes appear in the bread and cake chapters as a standard British domestic form.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze wrapped. Defrost at room temperature or toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: This loaf is better the day after baking. Store wrapped in baking paper and a cloth in a tin — it keeps for 5–7 days.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of soaking dried fruit in cold strong tea for several hours or overnight before baking. The fruit absorbs the liquid, plumping to roughly its original size and picking up the colour and tannins of the tea. The soaked fruit carries moisture into the baked loaf without the need for fat, producing a dense, moist crumb that keeps longer than a standard cake.

content.text: that starts the night before — the fruit must soak for at least 6 hours, overnight is better. The longer it soaks, the more liquid it absorbs, and the moister the finished loaf. The actual mixing takes about 5 minutes the next morning.

Brew two strong cups of black tea, leave to cool completely, then pour over the fruit. The cold temperature is deliberate — hot tea starts to cook the fruit and makes the soaking less effective. Any plain black tea works; builder's tea is fine.

Put {{sultanas}}, {{currants}}, {{raisins}}, and {{light-brown-sugar}} in a large bowl. Pour over 350 ml of cold strong black tea. Stir, cover, and leave overnight at room temperature — the fruit will absorb most of the liquid.

Heat the oven to 160°C (140°C fan). Line a 900 g (2 lb) loaf tin with baking paper. Beat the {{eggs}} into the soaked fruit, then stir in {{self-raising-flour}} and {{mixed-spice}} until just combined. The batter will be thick and sticky. Scrape into the tin, smooth the top, and bake for 55–65 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean. The top should be dark brown and firm. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes before turning out. Leave to cool completely before slicing.
